name: Jebel Ali Port
geopoliticalNotes: Largest man-made harbor and busiest port in the Middle East. Essential node of the Maritime Silk Road and major transshipment hub.
notes: Operated by DP World; integral to Jebel Ali Free Zone (JAFZA).

name: Khalifa Port
geopoliticalNotes: First semi-automated port in the region. Key for Abu Dhabi's Economic Vision 2030 and COSCO's Middle East hub.
notes: Linked to Khalifa Economic Zone Abu Dhabi (KEZAD).

name: Port of Fujairah
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic location outside the Strait of Hormuz. Second largest bunkering hub in the world and major oil export terminal.
notes: Connected to Habshan–Fujairah oil pipeline.

name: Khor Fakkan Port
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically located on the Gulf of Oman, bypassing the Strait of Hormuz. Major transshipment hub for Sharjah.
notes: Operated by Gulftainer on behalf of Sharjah Port Authority.

name: Port Rashid
geopoliticalNotes: Primary cruise destination in the Middle East. Retains strategic value for Ro-Ro and general cargo despite container move to Jebel Ali.
notes: Also known as Mina Rashid; home to the Queen Elizabeth 2 hotel.

name: Saqr Port
geopoliticalNotes: Largest bulk handling port in the Middle East. Critical for regional construction industry supply chains (aggregate and cement).
notes: Located in the industrial area of Khor Khwair.

name: Khalid Port
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic multipurpose gateway for Sharjah's industrial zones. Pioneer in regional container and Ro-Ro operations.
notes: Handles general cargo, reefer, and oil products.

name: Hamriyah Port
geopoliticalNotes: Critical hub for food security (livestock) and the oil and gas industry in the Northern Emirates.
notes: Adjacent to Hamriyah Free Zone.

name: Port of Buenos Aires
geopoliticalNotes: Nation's principal gateway for containerized trade and high-value imports. Connects Atlantic to Paraguay-Paraná river system.
notes: Multimodal hub and primary distribution center for the Southern Cone.

name: Port of Bahía Blanca
geopoliticalNotes: Premier petrochemical hub and natural deep-water port critical for large vessels. Primary terminal for energy resources (LNG/Crude).
notes: Major exit point for grains from the southern Pampas.

sources.name: Consorcio de Gestión del Puerto de Bahía Blanca

name: Port of San Lorenzo-San Martín
geopoliticalNotes: World's leading hub for soybean meal and oil exports. Heart of Argentina's agricultural export economy.
notes: Located on the Paraná River; part of a massive agro-industrial cluster.

sources.name: Cámara de Actividades Portuarias y Marítimas

name: Port of Rosario
geopoliticalNotes: Vital inland river port and key logistics node for central/northern agricultural regions.
notes: Secondary hub to San Lorenzo for grain exports.

name: Port of Quequén
geopoliticalNotes: Specialized in grains and oilseeds. Deep water allows large vessels to load fully without top-off.
notes: Critical link for southeastern agricultural production of Buenos Aires Province.

sources.name: Consorcio de Gestión del Puerto de Quequén

name: Port of La Plata
geopoliticalNotes: Major petrochemical and fuel hub, home to the country's largest refinery (YPF).
notes: TecPlata terminal provides a strategic alternative for container traffic.

sources.name: Consorcio de Gestión del Puerto de La Plata

name: Port of Mar del Plata
geopoliticalNotes: Leading fishing port accounting for nearly 45% of the national catch.
notes: Major tourism hub for cruise ships; undergoing modernization.

sources.name: Consorcio Portuario Regional de Mar del Plata

name: Port of Ushuaia
geopoliticalNotes: Gateway to Antarctica, handling 90% of global Antarctic cruise traffic. Critical for South Atlantic maritime security.
notes: Provides logistics for Tierra del Fuego special economic zone.

name: Chattogram Port
geopoliticalNotes: The 'Gateway to Bangladesh', handling over 90% of the country's international trade. Vital for the RMG (Ready-Made Garment) export sector.
notes: Includes the newly operational Patenga Container Terminal (PCT), managed by Red Sea Gateway Terminal.

name: Mongla Port
geopoliticalNotes: The second largest seaport in Bangladesh, serving as a primary hub for bulk cargo such as coal, cement, and vehicles. Key for trade with Nepal and Bhutan.
notes: Strategic focus on jute exports and car imports.

name: Payra Port
geopoliticalNotes: Bangladesh's third seaport, designed to accommodate larger vessels and serve the Payra Power Plant. A key component of the country's energy infrastructure.
notes: Full-scale container terminal operations expected by 2026.

name: Matarbari Deep Sea Port
geopoliticalNotes: Bangladesh's first deep-sea port, developed with Japanese assistance. Poised to become a regional transshipment hub for South Asia due to its extreme depth.
notes: Currently handling coal for the Matarbari Power Plant; commercial container operations scheduled for 2029.

name: Port of Dhaka (Sadarghat)
geopoliticalNotes: One of the busiest river ports in the world and the central logistics hub for the capital city. Critical for domestic distribution and passenger transport.
notes: Handles high volumes of consumer goods, salt, and cement for the capital.

name: Pangaon Inland Container Terminal
geopoliticalNotes: The first inland container terminal in Bangladesh, connecting Dhaka's manufacturing zones directly to the seaports via the Buriganga River.
notes: Designed to reduce congestion on the Dhaka-Chattogram highway.

name: Port of Narayanganj
geopoliticalNotes: A historic industrial port and the center for jute and textile logistics in Bangladesh. Vital for the domestic industrial supply chain.
notes: Handles bulk cargo including salt, cement, and industrial raw materials.

name: Ashuganj River Port
geopoliticalNotes: A critical transshipment hub for trade between Bangladesh and India's North-Eastern states. Key node in the Indo-Bangla Protocol on Inland Water Transit and Trade.
notes: Major transshipment point for Indian goods bound for Tripura and Assam.

name: Port of Santos
geopoliticalNotes: Largest port in Latin America, handling 30% of Brazil's trade. Primary gateway for the industrial heartland of São Paulo.
notes: Global hub for coffee, sugar, and soybeans.

name: Ponta da Madeira Maritime Terminal
geopoliticalNotes: Highest-tonnage port in Brazil. Critical exit point for the Carajás Mine iron ore, the world's largest.
notes: Operated by Vale; accommodates Valemax vessels.

name: Port of Tubarão
geopoliticalNotes: Major iron ore export hub serving the Iron Quadrangle in Minas Gerais.
notes: One of the most efficient mineral ports globally.

name: Port of Paranaguá
geopoliticalNotes: Largest bulk port in Latin America; critical 'Grain Gateway' for Brazil.
notes: Leads in soybean, corn, and vegetable oil exports.

name: Port of Itaqui
geopoliticalNotes: Cornerstone of the Arco Norte logistics corridor, providing efficient export routes for Midwest agricultural products.
notes: Major hub for liquid bulk (fuel) and minerals.

sources.name: EMAP - Empresa Maranhense de Administração Portuária

name: Port of Rio Grande
geopoliticalNotes: Southernmost major port, vital for Mercosur trade (Argentina, Uruguay, Paraguay).
notes: Specialized in agricultural commodities and fertilizers.

name: Port of Rio de Janeiro
geopoliticalNotes: Logistics hub for the Southeast region and critical support for the offshore oil and gas industry.
notes: Primary entry point for high-value manufactured imports.

sources.name: Companhia Docas do Rio de Janeiro

name: Port of Suape
geopoliticalNotes: Most important industrial port in the Northeast. Hub-and-spoke center for coastal shipping.
notes: Home to massive industrial complex including refineries and shipyards.

name: Port of Shanghai
geopoliticalNotes: The world's busiest container port for 14 consecutive years. It serves as the primary gateway for the Yangtze River Delta, China's most developed economic region, and is a central node in the Belt and Road Initiative.
notes: Home to the Yangshan Deep-Water Port, the world's largest automated container terminal, which is connected to the mainland by the 32km Donghai Bridge.

name: Port of Ningbo-Zhoushan
geopoliticalNotes: The world's largest port by total cargo tonnage. It is a critical hub for bulk commodities, including iron ore, crude oil, and coal, providing energy security and raw materials for China's industrial core.
notes: Its natural deep-water channels allow it to accommodate the world's largest 400,000-DWT ore carriers.

name: Port of Shenzhen
geopoliticalNotes: The primary export hub for China's high-tech and electronics industries. It is essential for global supply chains in consumer electronics.
notes: Includes major terminal areas Yantian, Shekou, and Chiwan.

name: Port of Qingdao
geopoliticalNotes: The leading gateway for Northern China and a major transshipment hub for Northeast Asia, particularly Japan and South Korea.
notes: A global pioneer in fully automated 'Smart Port' technology and 5G integration.

name: Port of Guangzhou
geopoliticalNotes: The largest comprehensive hub port in South China and a primary distribution center for automobiles and agricultural products (grain) in the Pearl River Delta.
notes: The Nansha Port Area is the only deep-water specialized container terminal on the west bank of the Pearl River.

name: Port of Tianjin
geopoliticalNotes: The maritime gateway to Beijing and Northern China's largest artificial port. It is vital for grain security and industrial logistics in the Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei region.
notes: Launched the world's first 'Smart Zero-Carbon' terminal in 2021.

name: Port of Xiamen
geopoliticalNotes: A critical hub for Cross-Strait trade with Taiwan and a major gateway for the '21st Century Maritime Silk Road' toward Southeast Asia.
notes: Known for advanced 5G-driven automated gantry cranes and high efficiency.

name: Port of Dalian
geopoliticalNotes: The primary ice-free port in Northeast China and a major naval base for the Northern Theater Command Navy. Home to China's primary aircraft carrier shipyard (DSIC).
notes: Handles 300,000+ DWT VLCCs and is a major hub for crude oil and iron ore in the North.

name: Port of Hamburg
geopoliticalNotes: Germany's largest port and the primary gateway for trade with Asia and Northern/Eastern Europe. Critical hub for the German industrial heartland.
notes: Third busiest container port in Europe. Known as the 'Gateway to the World'.

name: Port of Bremerhaven
geopoliticalNotes: A leading global automotive logistics hub and a major North Sea container terminal. Strategic for US military logistics in Europe.
notes: Part of the Bremen/Bremerhaven port group. One of the largest car ports in the world.

name: Port of Wilhelmshaven
geopoliticalNotes: Germany's only deep-water port (JadeWeserPort) and primary crude oil terminal. Expanding as a critical LNG and green energy hub.
notes: Deepest port in Germany, accommodating fully-laden Ultra Large Container Vessels (ULCVs).

name: Port of Rostock
geopoliticalNotes: Largest German Baltic port. Vital for Scandinavian trade and energy security (crude oil pipeline link to refineries).
notes: Major hub for liquid bulk and ferry traffic in the Baltic.

name: Port of Lübeck
geopoliticalNotes: Europe's largest Ro-Ro port on the Baltic. Critical for paper, pulp, and Scandinavian logistics.
notes: Historical Hanseatic hub, now specialized in modern Ro-Ro and forest product logistics.

name: Port of Brake
geopoliticalNotes: Specialized bulk port on the Weser. Germany's leading hub for agricultural products and cellulose.
notes: River port with significant specialized handling capacity for grain and animal feed.

name: Port of Brunsbüttel
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic location at the Kiel Canal entrance and Elbe mouth. Critical for the chemical industry and new LNG imports.
notes: Home to the Elbehafen and a new floating LNG terminal (FSRU).

name: Port of Emden
geopoliticalNotes: Europe's third-largest car terminal. Strategic export hub for the German automotive industry (Volkswagen).
notes: Major hub for offshore wind energy components and timber.

name: Alexandria Port
geopoliticalNotes: Egypt's main commercial gateway for over 2,000 years. Handles 60% of the country's foreign trade.
notes: Connected to the Mediterranean; major hub for general cargo and agricultural exports.

name: East Port Said
geopoliticalNotes: Premier global transshipment hub at the northern entrance of the Suez Canal. Central to the Suez Canal Economic Zone (SCZONE).
notes: Specifically designed for ultra-large container vessels (ULCVs).

name: West Port Said
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic location on the Mediterranean entrance of the Suez Canal; serves local trade and canal transit logistics.
notes: Handles general cargo and passenger traffic.

name: Damietta Port
geopoliticalNotes: Major Mediterranean hub for grain and LNG exports. Relieves pressure on Alexandria.
notes: Features a large dedicated LNG terminal.

name: Sokhna Port
geopoliticalNotes: Closest deep-water port to Cairo. Key part of the Suez Canal Economic Zone (SCZONE) on the Red Sea side.
notes: Operated by DP World Sokhna.

name: Safaga Port
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic for phosphate exports and pilgrim traffic to Saudi Arabia. Key Red Sea outlet for Upper Egypt.
notes: Developing as a major commercial hub for the 'Golden Triangle' project.

name: Abu Qir Port
geopoliticalNotes: Rapidly expanding naval/commercial hybrid port. Strategically deep draft to handle the next generation of container ships.
notes: Managed by the Egyptian Navy; undergoing massive expansion.

name: Adabiya Port
geopoliticalNotes: Specializes in oil, chemicals, and bulk cargo. Strategic location near the southern entrance of the Suez Canal.
notes: Located on the western shore of the Gulf of Suez.

name: Port of Marseille-Fos
geopoliticalNotes: France's largest port and a leading Mediterranean hub. Critical energy gateway (oil and LNG) and primary link to North Africa.
notes: Divided into the Eastern Basins (Marseille city) and Western Basins (Fos-sur-Mer).

name: Port of Le Havre
geopoliticalNotes: France's premier container port and the main Atlantic gateway for the Paris region. Part of the HAROPA alliance.
notes: Port 2000 terminal can accommodate the largest container ships in the world.

name: Port of Dunkirk
geopoliticalNotes: Major industrial port on the North Sea. Strategic hub for steel (ArcelorMittal) and the emerging EV battery industry.
notes: Third largest French port by tonnage. Deep-water capabilities for dry bulk.

name: Port of Calais
geopoliticalNotes: Europe's leading port for cross-Channel Ro-Ro traffic. Vital link for just-in-time logistics between the UK and continental Europe.
notes: Handles high volumes of accompanied freight and passenger traffic.

name: Port of Nantes Saint-Nazaire
geopoliticalNotes: Major Atlantic hub for aerospace, shipbuilding (Chantiers de l'Atlantique), and energy (LNG terminal at Montoir-de-Bretagne).
notes: Strategically located at the mouth of the Loire estuary.

name: Port of Rouen
geopoliticalNotes: Europe's leading center for cereal exports. Critical link in the Seine Axis logistics chain connecting to Paris.
notes: Maritime river port located 80 km from the sea. Part of HAROPA Port.

name: Port of La Rochelle
geopoliticalNotes: Only deep-water port on the French Atlantic coast not on an estuary. Strategic for agri-bulk and renewable energy components.
notes: Known as Grand Port Maritime de la Pallice.

name: Port of Bordeaux
geopoliticalNotes: Major multimodal hub in Southwest France handling cereals, hydrocarbons, and chemicals. Strategic for the regional wine and aerospace economy.
notes: Operates across seven terminals along the Gironde estuary.

name: Port of Felixstowe
geopoliticalNotes: The UK's largest and busiest container port, handling nearly 50% of the country's containerized trade. Primary gateway for Asian imports.
notes: Deepest water container port in the UK. Managed by Hutchison Ports.

name: DP World London Gateway
geopoliticalNotes: State-of-the-art automated terminal serving the London metropolitan area. Part of the Thames Freeport.
notes: Throughput and tonnage combined with Tilbury as Port of London hub. Highly automated operations.

name: Port of Immingham
geopoliticalNotes: The UK's largest port by tonnage, critical for energy security (biomass, coal, and oil) and industrial supply chains in the North.
notes: Part of the ABP Humber cluster. Major hub for the UK's energy transition.

name: Port of Southampton
geopoliticalNotes: Busiest cruise port in Europe and leading hub for automotive exports (Rolls-Royce, MINI). Critical for trade with North America.
notes: Operated by ABP. Second largest container port in the UK.

name: Port of Liverpool
geopoliticalNotes: Primary gateway for transatlantic trade. Liverpool2 terminal enables handling of largest container ships on the West coast.
notes: Managed by Peel Ports. Vital hub for North West industrial cluster.

name: Port of Milford Haven
geopoliticalNotes: The UK's largest energy port. Handles 25% of UK petrol/diesel and 30% of gas requirements via major LNG terminals.
notes: Home to South Hook LNG, one of the largest LNG terminals in the world.

name: Port of Teesport
geopoliticalNotes: Major northern logistics hub with Teesside Freeport status. Strategic for steel, chemicals, and retail logistics.
notes: Operated by PD Ports. Largest port in the North East.

name: Port of Dover
geopoliticalNotes: The world's busiest ferry port. Most vital link for just-in-time supply chains between the UK and EU, handling £144bn in trade.
notes: Primarily Ro-Ro freight. Critical sensitivity to post-Brexit customs and border security.

name: Port of Tanjung Priok
geopoliticalNotes: Indonesia's primary international gateway, handling over 50% of the nation's transshipment cargo. Key to the Global Maritime Fulcrum strategy.
notes: Ongoing Kalibaru expansion (New Priok) aims to increase capacity to 12 million TEUs.

name: Port of Tanjung Perak
geopoliticalNotes: The main logistics hub for Eastern Indonesia, connecting Java with Sulawesi, Maluku, and Papua.
notes: Includes the automated Teluk Lamong Terminal.

name: Port of Belawan
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically located at the mouth of the Malacca Strait, serving as Sumatra's primary export hub for palm oil and rubber.
notes: Recently expanded with the Belawan New Container Terminal (BNCT).

name: Makassar New Port
geopoliticalNotes: Gateway to Eastern Indonesia and a critical node in the trans-Pacific shipping routes. Designed to bypass Java for direct exports.
notes: Phase 1 was inaugurated in 2024; it is the deepest port in Eastern Indonesia.

name: Batu Ampar Port
geopoliticalNotes: Located in the Batam Free Trade Zone directly across from Singapore, serving as a competitive transshipment and industrial hub in the Malacca Strait.
notes: Strategic focus on competing with Singapore for regional transshipment.

name: Patimban International Port
geopoliticalNotes: Developed with Japanese investment to decongest Tanjung Priok and support the Rebana industrial estate, particularly for automotive exports.
notes: Strategic focus on the automotive industry supply chain.

name: Kuala Tanjung Port
geopoliticalNotes: Integrated with the Sei Mangkei SEZ, intended to be a major international hub in Western Indonesia to rival regional ports in Malaysia and Singapore.
notes: Strategic location on the Malacca Strait with deep-water capabilities.

name: Port of Tanjung Emas
geopoliticalNotes: The primary logistics hub for Central Java's export-oriented manufacturing (furniture, textiles). Highly vulnerable to land subsidence and tidal flooding.
notes: Critical for Central Java's industrial zones.

name: Mundra Port
geopoliticalNotes: India's largest commercial port and a primary gateway for Northern India. First Indian port to handle over 200 MMT of cargo in a fiscal year.
notes: Privately operated by Adani Ports and SEZ Limited (APSEZ).

name: Jawaharlal Nehru Port (JNPT)
geopoliticalNotes: India's premier container hub, handling over 50% of the country's containerized trade. Critical node for the International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC).
notes: Also known as Nhava Sheva. Fully landlord-based major port.

name: Deendayal Port
geopoliticalNotes: A major hub for crude oil, chemicals, and food grains. Serves the industrial hinterland of North-Western India.
notes: Formerly known as Kandla Port. A major government-owned port.

sources.name: Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways

name: Paradip Port
geopoliticalNotes: Top cargo-handling government port in FY2024-25. Specialized in bulk commodities like iron ore and coal, critical for India's steel industry.
notes: Located on the confluence of the Mahanadi River and the Bay of Bengal.

name: Visakhapatnam Port
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic deep-water port hosting the Eastern Naval Command. Critical for iron ore exports to East Asia and handling strategic oil reserves.
notes: Deepest landlocked port in India.

name: Chennai Port
geopoliticalNotes: Known as the 'Gateway to South India'. A major hub for automobile exports (Detroit of Asia) and containerized trade on the East Coast.
notes: Second largest container port in India after JNPT.

name: Syama Prasad Mookerjee Port
geopoliticalNotes: India's only major riverine port. Vital for trade with landlocked Nepal and Bhutan and the North-Eastern states of India.
notes: Consists of Kolkata Dock System and Haldia Dock Complex.

name: Cochin Port
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically located on the East-West international sea route. Home to India's first dedicated transshipment terminal (Vallarpadam) and a major naval base.
notes: Key for energy imports and maritime security in the Indian Ocean.

name: Shahid Rajaee Port
geopoliticalNotes: Iran's largest container hub, handling ~85% of the country's container traffic. Primary gateway for the INSTC Western Route.
notes: Strategically located near the Strait of Hormuz. Essential for national trade and regional logistics.

sources.name: Ports and Maritime Organization of Iran

name: Imam Khomeini Port
geopoliticalNotes: Main entry point for Iran's food security (grain imports) and a critical node for the petrochemical industry.
notes: Handles the majority of 'essential goods' and bulk commodities for the country.

sources.name: Ports and Maritime Organization of Iran

name: Chabahar Port (Shahid Beheshti)
geopoliticalNotes: Iran's only oceanic port, providing a bypass to the Strait of Hormuz. Primary gateway for India to reach Afghanistan and Central Asia.
notes: Operated under a 10-year contract by India. Significant potential for transshipment in the Indian Ocean.

sources.name: Ports and Maritime Organization of Iran

name: Bushehr Port
geopoliticalNotes: Main export hub for trade with Arab nations, specifically Saudi Arabia and Qatar. Strategically positioned in the central Persian Gulf.
notes: Undergoing dredging to accommodate 50,000-ton vessels.

sources.name: Ports and Maritime Organization of Iran

name: Anzali Port
geopoliticalNotes: Key Caspian port for trade with Russia and CIS countries. Directly connected to the national railway as of 2024.
notes: Central to the INSTC Caspian route.

sources.name: Ports and Maritime Organization of Iran

name: Amirabad Port
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic hub for grain imports in the Caspian Sea. Integral to the INSTC Eastern Route.
notes: Largest port on the Caspian Sea's southern coast with significant silo capacity.

sources.name: Ports and Maritime Organization of Iran

name: Pars Special Economic Energy Zone Port
geopoliticalNotes: Energy hub for the South Pars gas field, the world's largest gas field. Vital for Iran's LNG and petrochemical exports.
notes: Highly specialized for gas and liquid bulk products.

name: Kharg Island Oil Terminal
geopoliticalNotes: Iran's primary crude oil export terminal, handling over 90% of the country's oil exports.
notes: Deep-water terminal capable of handling ULCCs. Highly strategic for national revenue.

name: Haifa Port
geopoliticalNotes: Israel's largest and most efficient gateway. Recently privatized by Adani Group; major hub for containers and cruise tourism.
notes: Houses the main Israeli Navy base; critical for national security.

name: Ashdod Port
geopoliticalNotes: Primary gateway for consumer imports (60% of goods). Critical for humanitarian aid to Gaza and proximity to Israel's industrial heartland.
notes: State-owned port undergoing modernization to compete with new private terminals.

name: Eilat Port
geopoliticalNotes: Israel's only Red Sea outlet; strategic 'back door' bypassing the Suez Canal. Vital for vehicle imports and potash exports.
notes: Currently heavily impacted by regional maritime instability in the Red Sea.

name: Bay Port (Haifa)
geopoliticalNotes: Operated by SIPG (China); Israel's most advanced and eco-friendly terminal. Capable of handling ULCVs.
notes: New terminal opened in 2021 to introduce competition in Haifa.

name: Hadarom Port
geopoliticalNotes: Semi-automated terminal operated by TiL. Expands Ashdod's capacity to handle the world's largest container ships.
notes: Also known as the South Port; key to Israel's port reform.

name: Hadera Port
geopoliticalNotes: Critical energy port serving the Orot Rabin Power Plant. Essential for Israel's power supply infrastructure.
notes: Primarily handles coal and fuel products via deep-water jetty.

name: Ashkelon Port
geopoliticalNotes: Vital energy hub housing EAPC pipeline infrastructure. Key node for oil trade between Europe and the Far East.
notes: Located south of Ashkelon; major player in domestic fuel security.

name: Haifa Naval Base
geopoliticalNotes: Main base of the Israeli Navy and home to the country's submarine fleet. Essential for Mediterranean maritime security.
notes: Restricted military area adjacent to the commercial Haifa Port.

name: Port of Tokyo
geopoliticalNotes: Primary maritime gateway for the Greater Tokyo Area and Japan's consumer hub.
notes: Key node for domestic and international distribution; resilient infrastructure.

sources.name: Bureau of Port and Harbor, Tokyo Metropolitan Government

name: Port of Nagoya
geopoliticalNotes: Japan's largest port by cargo tonnage; global export heart for the automotive industry (Toyota).
notes: Vital for the Chūbu industrial region and national economic health.

name: Port of Yokohama
geopoliticalNotes: Deepest port in Japan, capable of handling 24,000 TEU mega-vessels.
notes: Ranked as the world's most efficient port; critical for Keihin industrial zone.

sources.name: City of Yokohama Port and Harbor Bureau

name: Port of Kobe
geopoliticalNotes: Premier deep-water natural harbor; resilient transshipment hub for Western Japan.
notes: Part of the Hanshin International Strategic Port cluster.

name: Port of Osaka
geopoliticalNotes: Primary gateway for trade with China and Southeast Asia.
notes: A major commercial hub for the Kansai region.

name: Port of Chiba
geopoliticalNotes: Japan's energy lifeline; handles the highest volume of raw materials including LNG and crude oil.
notes: Vital for the Keiyō Industrial Zone's energy security.

name: Port of Kitakyushu
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic 'Gateway to Asia' due to its proximity to South Korea and China.
notes: Vital link between Kyushu and Honshu; heavy industrial support.

sources.name: Kitakyushu City Port and Harbor Bureau

name: Port of Kawasaki
geopoliticalNotes: Core industrial hub in the Keihin zone; home to Japan's largest refrigerated warehouse complex.
notes: Critical for food security and energy supply in the Kanto region.

name: Port of Mombasa
geopoliticalNotes: Principal gateway for East and Central Africa, serving the Northern Corridor (Uganda, Rwanda, DRC). Vital for regional trade stability.
notes: Ongoing expansion of berths 23 and 24 to increase container capacity.

name: Port of Lamu
geopoliticalNotes: Part of the LAPSSET corridor aimed at connecting Kenya, South Sudan, and Ethiopia. Designed for post-Panamax vessels.
notes: First three berths operational; planned to expand to 32 berths.

name: Port of Shimoni
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically located near the Tanzanian border; being developed into a modern fishing and commercial port.
notes: Transformation into a large-scale commercial port is ongoing as of 2025.

name: Port of Malindi
geopoliticalNotes: Serves the local tourism and fishing industries; limited commercial cargo capacity.
notes: Primarily a jetty for small vessels and fishing boats.

name: Port of Kilifi
geopoliticalNotes: Local port serving agriculture and limestone industries in the coastal region.
notes: Significant for regional logistics but limited in scale.

name: Port of Mtwapa
geopoliticalNotes: Minor port used for local trade and fishing.
notes: Limited infrastructure for large-scale maritime operations.

name: Port of Kiunga
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic location near the Somali border; critical for security monitoring and coastal surveillance.
notes: Small port primarily used for local trade and security operations.

name: Port of Funzi
geopoliticalNotes: Small island port used for local transport and eco-tourism.
notes: Minimal infrastructure, serving local community needs.

name: Port of Busan
geopoliticalNotes: World's 6th busiest container port and 2nd largest transshipment hub; primary gateway for Northeast Asian trade.
notes: Includes Busan New Port and North Port; critical for global shipping alliances.

name: Port of Incheon
geopoliticalNotes: Primary maritime gateway to the Seoul Metropolitan Area; central hub for trade with China.
notes: Ongoing expansion at Incheon New Port; major hub for e-commerce.

name: Port of Gwangyang
geopoliticalNotes: Specialized industrial powerhouse supporting POSCO Steel Works and Yeosu National Industrial Complex.
notes: South Korea's leading port for dry and liquid bulk shipments.

name: Port of Ulsan
geopoliticalNotes: Known as the 'Oil Capital,' handles over 30% of the nation's liquid cargo; home to world's largest automobile export terminal.
notes: Major global shipbuilding hub (Hyundai Heavy Industries).

name: Port of Pyeongtaek-Dangjin
geopoliticalNotes: Closest Korean port to China's eastern economic zones; primary hub for automobile imports/exports.
notes: Fastest-growing port in South Korea; critical for LNG supply to the capital region.

sources.name: Pyeongtaek Regional Office of Oceans and Fisheries

name: Port of Pohang
geopoliticalNotes: The 'Steel Industry Lifeline' developed to support POSCO's steel production.
notes: Main industrial gateway for the Daegu-Gyeongbuk inland region.

sources.name: Pohang Regional Office of Oceans and Fisheries

name: Port of Gunsan
geopoliticalNotes: Key industrial and export hub on the Yellow Sea; benefits from a Free Trade Zone.
notes: Focuses on heavy machinery, grain, and proximity to Saemangeum project.

sources.name: Gunsan Regional Office of Oceans and Fisheries

name: Port of Mokpo
geopoliticalNotes: Southwestern gateway for cargo and passenger traffic between Korea, China, and Japan.
notes: Vital for the Daebul Industrial Complex, specializing in ship blocks.

sources.name: Mokpo Regional Office of Oceans and Fisheries

name: Port of Manzanillo
geopoliticalNotes: Mexico's 'Pacific Gateway' and busiest port. Primary entry point for Asian goods destined for Mexico City and the industrial heartland.
notes: Handles over 40% of Mexico's total container traffic.

name: Port of Lázaro Cárdenas
geopoliticalNotes: Deepest port in Mexico; capable of handling Post-Panamax vessels. Strategic alternative to congested U.S. West Coast ports.
notes: Critical industrial hub for steel and minerals.

name: Port of Veracruz
geopoliticalNotes: Primary Atlantic gateway for trade with Europe and U.S. East Coast. Historically the most significant port in Mexico.
notes: Leading automotive hub; handles the majority of vehicle imports/exports.

name: Port of Altamira
geopoliticalNotes: Massive industrial-port complex specializing in petrochemicals and LNG. Serves northern industrial states like Nuevo León.
notes: Key node for the USMCA corridor.

name: Port of Ensenada
geopoliticalNotes: Vital for the 'maquiladora' industry in northern Mexico due to proximity to the U.S. border.
notes: Provides direct link to Asian markets for northern Mexico; major cruise destination.

name: Port of Progreso
geopoliticalNotes: Primary gateway to the Yucatán Peninsula; features one of the world's longest piers.
notes: Essential for regional tourism, agricultural exports, and hydrocarbon distribution.

name: Port of Tuxpan
geopoliticalNotes: Closest Gulf port to Mexico City; highly automated and critical for fuel imports.
notes: Rapid road connectivity to the capital makes it vital for the automotive industry.

name: Port of Salina Cruz
geopoliticalNotes: Cornerstone of the Interoceanic Corridor of the Isthmus of Tehuantepec (CIIT); potential Panama Canal alternative.
notes: Primarily handles bulk and oil currently; major development underway.

name: Port Klang
geopoliticalNotes: Malaysia's primary gateway and the 10th busiest container port in the world; major transshipment hub connecting Asia with Europe.
notes: Includes Westport, Northport, and Southpoint; National Load Centre.

name: Port of Tanjung Pelepas (PTP)
geopoliticalNotes: Premier global transshipment hub and main competitor to the Port of Singapore; key Asian hub for the Gemini Cooperation.
notes: Strategically located at the confluence of the Malacca and Singapore Straits.

name: Penang Port
geopoliticalNotes: Primary gateway for the Northern Corridor Economic Region (NCER) and Southern Thailand.
notes: Vital link in the Indonesia-Malaysia-Thailand Growth Triangle (IMT-GT).

name: Johor Port (Pasir Gudang)
geopoliticalNotes: World’s largest palm oil terminal and major regional commodity hub; designated London Metal Exchange (LME) location.
notes: Primary industrial port for Southern Peninsular Malaysia.

name: Bintulu Port
geopoliticalNotes: Malaysia's principal LNG export hub and one of the largest LNG terminals in the world.
notes: Primary maritime gateway for East Malaysia and the BIMP-EAGA region.

name: Kuantan Port
geopoliticalNotes: Main gateway to the East Coast of Peninsular Malaysia; key node in the 21st Century Maritime Silk Road.
notes: Supported by significant Chinese investment; new deep water terminal capacity.

name: Sapangar Bay Container Port
geopoliticalNotes: Premier transshipment hub for the BIMP-EAGA region, facilitating trade in the Sulu-Celebes Seas.
notes: Positioned to capture growing trade in the East ASEAN Growth Area.

name: Kemaman Port
geopoliticalNotes: Specialized industrial port serving the Oil & Gas industry and heavy manufacturing on the East Coast.
notes: Features some of the deepest water depths in Malaysia; critical for steel and petrochemicals.

name: Tanger Med
geopoliticalNotes: The largest port in the Mediterranean and Africa. Strategically located on the Strait of Gibraltar, it serves as a primary global transshipment hub connecting Africa, Europe, and Asia.
notes: Handles more than 50% of Morocco's exports and is a key driver of the national economy.

name: Port of Valencia
geopoliticalNotes: Spain's leading container port and a major gateway for the Western Mediterranean. Handles over 50% of Spain's GDP-generating maritime trade.
notes: Key logistics hub for the Iberian Peninsula and Southern Europe.

name: Port of Piraeus
geopoliticalNotes: The 'Gateway to Europe' for Chinese trade (COSCO managed). Largest passenger port in Europe and a vital hub for the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans.
notes: Critical node in the 21st Century Maritime Silk Road.

name: Port of Algeciras
geopoliticalNotes: Pure transshipment powerhouse at the crossroads of the Mediterranean and Atlantic. Strategic for global East-West and North-South routes.
notes: One of the largest ports in Europe by total tonnage. Critical bunker fuel hub.

name: Port Said
geopoliticalNotes: Located at the northern entrance of the Suez Canal. Essential for all vessels transiting between Asia and Europe. Highly strategic for global trade flow.
notes: East Port Said is the primary container hub, while West Port Said handles general cargo and transit.

name: Port of Gioia Tauro
geopoliticalNotes: Italy's largest container port and a dedicated transshipment hub in the heart of the Mediterranean. Nexus for regional feeder routes.
notes: Strategically located for redistributing cargo across the central and eastern Mediterranean.

name: Port of Barcelona
geopoliticalNotes: Diversified logistics hub and the main gateway for the Iberian Peninsula and Southern France. Strong focus on high-value cargo and vehicles.
notes: Leading cruise port in the Mediterranean and Europe.

name: Malta Freeport
geopoliticalNotes: Highly specialized transshipment hub leverage Malta's central location. 96% of traffic is pure transshipment, redistributing cargo across the Mediterranean and Black Sea.
notes: Crucial hub for major shipping lines like CMA CGM and MSC in the central Mediterranean.

name: Lekki Deep Sea Port
geopoliticalNotes: Nigeria's most advanced and deepest port. Capable of handling mega-ships up to 14,500 TEUs. Regional hub for West Africa.
notes: Integrated with Lagos Free Zone and Dangote Refinery complex.

name: Onne Port Complex
geopoliticalNotes: Primary hub for Nigeria's oil and gas sector. Houses the world's largest Oil and Gas Free Zone.
notes: Handles over 65% of Nigeria's export cargo excluding crude oil.

name: Lagos Port Complex (Apapa)
geopoliticalNotes: Nigeria's 'Premiere Port' and most critical gateway for the commercial capital. Best rail connectivity in the country.
notes: Handles the highest value of non-oil trade.

name: Tin Can Island Port
geopoliticalNotes: Nigeria's second busiest port, specialized in RORO (Roll-on/Roll-off) cargo.
notes: Primary entry point for imported vehicles and general merchandise.

name: Rivers Port Complex
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic for coal export and refined petroleum product import. Logistics base for Eastern industrial heartlands.
notes: One of the oldest ports in Nigeria.

name: Delta Port
geopoliticalNotes: Critical hub for Escravos Gas-to-Liquids (EGTL) projects and regional oil refineries.
notes: Declared a Free Trade Zone in 2011 to stimulate industrial growth.

name: Calabar Port Complex
geopoliticalNotes: Closest maritime gateway to 16 Northern states and landlocked neighbors Chad and Niger.
notes: Draft limitations restrict its throughput compared to Lagos ports.

name: Ibom Deep Sea Port
geopoliticalNotes: Under development; poised to be a game-changer for the Gulf of Guinea. Features a natural draft of 18m.
notes: Designed as a transshipment hub to serve West and Central Africa.

name: Port of Rotterdam
geopoliticalNotes: Europe's largest port and the 'Gateway to Europe.' It is a critical node for NATO logistics and European energy security, handling over 35% of all North Range shipping. It is central to the Rhine-Meuse-Scheldt delta economy.
notes: Home to Maasvlakte 2, featuring world-leading automated container terminals. It is also a premier energy hub with five refineries and major LNG facilities.

name: Port of Amsterdam
geopoliticalNotes: The world's largest gasoline port and a critical component of the ARA (Amsterdam-Rotterdam-Antwerp) energy trading hub. It plays a vital role in global petroleum product markets.
notes: Currently transitioning from coal to become a major hub for biomass and green hydrogen. Home to the world's largest sea lock (Zeesluis IJmuiden).

name: Port of Vlissingen
geopoliticalNotes: A key 'Host Nation Support' port for US and NATO military deployments in Europe. It is also a primary hub for the assembly and maintenance of North Sea offshore wind farms.
notes: Part of the cross-border North Sea Port (shared with Terneuzen and Ghent). Strategically located directly on the North Sea.

name: Port of Terneuzen
geopoliticalNotes: A major hub for the European chemical industry and the gateway to the Ghent-Terneuzen Canal. It is essential for the logistics of the industrial clusters in the Scheldt delta.
notes: Part of the North Sea Port. It handles significant volumes of dry and liquid bulk for regional industrial processing.

name: Port of Moerdijk
geopoliticalNotes: The furthest inland seaport in the Netherlands with deep-sea access. It serves as a vital multimodal link between the ports of Rotterdam and Antwerp and is a major center for chemical recycling.
notes: An essential node in the European inland waterway network, providing efficient hinterland connections.

name: Port of Eemshaven
geopoliticalNotes: A critical energy hub for the Netherlands and Northwest Europe. It hosts the EemsEnergyTerminal (LNG), which was rapidly deployed to replace Russian gas imports, and is the landing point for major trans-Atlantic data cables.
notes: The primary logistics and O&M base for German and Dutch offshore wind farms in the North Sea.

name: Port of Delfzijl
geopoliticalNotes: A major chemical cluster in Northern Netherlands, integrated with the regional energy transition efforts. It is specialized in green chemistry and the circular economy.
notes: Part of Groningen Seaports. It provides critical industrial capacity for the Northern Dutch economy.

name: Port of Den Helder
geopoliticalNotes: The main home port of the Royal Netherlands Navy and a critical offshore service hub for the North Sea oil, gas, and wind sectors. It is essential for national defense and offshore energy maintenance.
notes: Includes the historic Willemsoord naval yard and is the primary 'pit-stop' for vessels operating in the North Sea energy sectors.

sources.name: Port of Den Helder Official Website

name: Karachi Port
geopoliticalNotes: Pakistan's busiest and oldest seaport, handling approximately 60% of the nation's total trade. Highly strategic for national economic security.
notes: Includes the Pakistan Deep Water Container Terminal (PDWCT) operated by Hutchison Ports.

name: Port Muhammad Bin Qasim
geopoliticalNotes: Pakistan's second largest port and the primary hub for the country's energy sector, handling LNG, coal, and industrial raw materials.
notes: Home to the Qasim International Container Terminal (QICT) and multiple dedicated energy terminals.

name: Gwadar Port
geopoliticalNotes: The flagship project of the China-Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC). A deep-sea port designed to provide China with a direct link to the Arabian Sea, bypassing the Malacca Strait.
notes: Managed by China Overseas Port Holding Company (COPHC). Strategic focus on regional transshipment.

name: Jinnah Naval Base
geopoliticalNotes: The second largest naval base of the Pakistan Navy, providing strategic depth and a secure alternative to the Karachi naval base. Critical for maritime defense and logistics.
notes: Offers a secure deep-water harbor for submarines and surface vessels.

name: Pasni Port
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically located between Gwadar and Ormara. Proposed as a major hub for mineral exports (copper, antimony) to international markets.
notes: Currently primarily a fishing harbor; undergoing significant expansion for industrial use.

name: Jiwani Port
geopoliticalNotes: Located near the Iranian border and the mouth of the Gulf of Oman. Highly strategic for monitoring maritime traffic entering the Strait of Hormuz.
notes: Small harbor with significant naval and surveillance importance.

name: Keti Bandar Port
geopoliticalNotes: A historic port being revived to serve as an energy hub, specifically for the export of Thar coal and the development of power plants.
notes: Strategic focus on national energy security and coal-based industries.

name: Korangi Fish Harbour
geopoliticalNotes: Pakistan's primary seafood export hub, critical for the country's blue economy and foreign exchange earnings from fish exports.
notes: Undergoing modernization to meet international sanitary and phytosanitary standards.

name: Port of Manila
geopoliticalNotes: The primary international gateway of the Philippines, handling the vast majority of the country's foreign trade. Highly strategic for national economic security.
notes: Comprises the Manila International Container Terminal (MICT), Manila South Harbor, and Manila North Harbor.

name: Port of Cebu
geopoliticalNotes: The largest domestic hub in the Philippines and the primary gateway for the Visayas region. Central to the country's inter-island logistics network.
notes: The New Cebu International Container Port (NCICP) is under development to handle larger vessels.

name: Port of Batangas
geopoliticalNotes: A critical alternate gateway to Manila, handling significant volumes of petroleum products, automotive imports, and RoRo traffic connecting Luzon to Mindoro and Visayas.
notes: Home to major oil refineries and car terminal operations.

name: Port of Subic Bay
geopoliticalNotes: A former US naval base with deep-water capabilities. Highly strategic for military logistics and as a growing industrial/logistics hub in Northern Luzon.
notes: Managed by the Subic Bay Metropolitan Authority (SBMA).

name: Port of Davao
geopoliticalNotes: The main gateway for Southern Mindanao, critical for the global export of agricultural products (bananas, pineapples). Key node in the BIMP-EAGA corridor.
notes: Includes the Sasa Wharf and the privately-run Davao International Container Terminal (DICT).

name: Port of Cagayan de Oro
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically located in Northern Mindanao, serving as a primary transit point for industrial and agricultural goods for the entire island.
notes: Often referred to as the 'Global Gateway to Mindanao'.

name: Port of General Santos
geopoliticalNotes: Vial for the tuna industry and agricultural exports of the SOCCSKSARGEN region. Strategic for connectivity to Indonesia and Australia.
notes: Also known as Makar Wharf.

name: Port of Iloilo
geopoliticalNotes: A key trade and commercial hub for Western Visayas, serving the sugar industry and food security for the region.
notes: Includes the Iloilo Commercial Port Complex (ICPC).

name: Port of Gdańsk
geopoliticalNotes: Poland's largest port and the second-largest in the Baltic Sea. Critical energy gateway (Naftoport) and major container hub for Central and Eastern Europe.
notes: Home to the Baltic Hub (formerly DCT Gdańsk), the largest container terminal in the Baltic.

name: Port of Gdynia
geopoliticalNotes: A universal port and Poland's primary gateway for grain exports. Strategic naval base and container terminal.
notes: Major investments ongoing to deepen the port to 15.5m to accommodate larger vessels.

name: Port of Świnoujście
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically vital for energy security due to the Lech Kaczyński LNG Terminal. Major ferry link to Scandinavia.
notes: Part of the Szczecin-Świnoujście port complex. Key to diversifying gas supplies away from Russia.

name: Port of Szczecin
geopoliticalNotes: Major inland seaport located at the mouth of the Oder. Strategic hub for bulk cargo and general goods for the Western Polish and East German markets.
notes: Recently deepened fairway from Świnoujście allows much larger ships to reach the port.

name: Port of Police
geopoliticalNotes: Specialized industrial port serving the Police Chemical Works. Critical for fertilizer and chemical exports.
notes: Poland's fourth busiest port by tonnage. Deep-water sea terminal for raw material imports.

name: Port of Kołobrzeg
geopoliticalNotes: Leading regional port on the central coast. Strategic for fishing, local commerce, and seasonal passenger traffic.
notes: Combines commercial, fishing, and yachting functions.

name: Port of Elbląg
geopoliticalNotes: Gained strategic importance following the opening of the Vistula Spit canal, allowing access to the Baltic bypassing Russian-controlled Pilawa Strait.
notes: Targeting 2-3 million tonnes of throughput following planned infrastructure upgrades.

name: Port of Darłowo
geopoliticalNotes: Regional commercial and fishing port. Strategic for agricultural and timber exports from the central Pomeranian region.
notes: Located at the mouth of the Wieprza river.

name: Port of Novorossiysk
geopoliticalNotes: Russia's largest port and a key hub for grain, fertilizer, and oil exports via the Black Sea. Central to the International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC).
notes: Significant for military logistics in the Black Sea region. Non-freezing deep-water port.

name: Port of Ust-Luga
geopoliticalNotes: Largest Baltic port for Russia; primary outlet for energy products and fertilizers. Essential for bypassing transit through Baltic states.
notes: Deep-water port capable of handling large-capacity vessels. Expanding rapidly to handle diverted cargo.

name: Port of Vostochny
geopoliticalNotes: Key coal and container gateway to the Asia-Pacific region. Integral to Russia's 'Pivot to the East' and the Trans-Siberian railway logistics.
notes: Undergoing expansion to 70M tonnes capacity by 2026.

name: Port of Murmansk
geopoliticalNotes: Ice-free Arctic port; crucial for the Northern Sea Route (NSR) and military operations in the North Atlantic.
notes: New Lavna coal terminal added 18M t capacity in 2025.

name: Port of Primorsk
geopoliticalNotes: Largest oil export terminal in the Baltic Sea; critical for Russian crude and petroleum product exports to world markets.
notes: Vulnerable to security threats in the Baltic region. Recent infrastructure damage reported in 2026.

name: Port of Vladivostok
geopoliticalNotes: Russia's primary container hub in the Far East. Main link for trade with China, South Korea, and Japan.
notes: Central to FESCO's Asia-Pacific logistics network.

name: Port of St. Petersburg
geopoliticalNotes: Historic gateway to Europe; remains a major multi-purpose hub despite shifting trade patterns away from the West.
notes: Large concentration of berths and logistical infrastructure.

name: Port of Taman
geopoliticalNotes: Deep-water port for coal, grain, and LPG; critical for bypassing Bosphorus constraints and increasing export capacity.
notes: Non-freezing port with deep-water berths for large bulk carriers.

name: Jeddah Islamic Port
geopoliticalNotes: Primary gateway to the Kingdom and busiest port on the Red Sea. Crucial for pilgrims and trade between Europe, Asia, and Africa.
notes: Handles 65% of Saudi Arabia's sea imports.

name: King Abdulaziz Port
geopoliticalNotes: Largest port on the Arabian Gulf. Connected to Riyadh Dry Port by rail; primary export hub for oil and petrochemicals.
notes: Key gateway for the Eastern and Central regions.

name: King Abdullah Port
geopoliticalNotes: First privately owned and operated port in the Middle East. Strategic node in King Abdullah Economic City.
notes: Consistently ranked as one of the most efficient ports globally.

name: Jubail Commercial Port
geopoliticalNotes: Supports the world's largest petrochemical complex in Jubail Industrial City.
notes: Recently expanded to handle mega-ships.

name: King Fahad Industrial Port (Jubail)
geopoliticalNotes: The world's largest industrial port. Vital for Saudi Arabia's energy and petrochemical exports on the Arabian Gulf.
notes: Primary export point for SABIC products.

name: King Fahad Industrial Port (Yanbu)
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic alternative for oil exports to bypass the Strait of Hormuz. Terminus for the East-West Pipeline.
notes: Largest oil export port on the Red Sea.

name: Yanbu Commercial Port
geopoliticalNotes: Maritime gateway for Medina and Al-Qassim. Important for food security (grain/livestock imports).
notes: One of the oldest ports on the Red Sea.

name: Ras Al Khair Port
geopoliticalNotes: Dedicated mining port linked by rail to northern mines. Vital for phosphate and aluminum exports.
notes: Serves the Ras Al Khair Industrial City.

name: Tuas Port
geopoliticalNotes: Designed to be the world's largest fully automated container terminal upon completion in the 2040s. It represents Singapore's long-term strategy to maintain its status as a global transshipment hub by consolidating all container operations into a single, high-tech mega-port.
notes: Currently in Phase 1 of operation. The port utilizes automated quay cranes and AI-driven vessel traffic management.

sources.name: Maritime and Port Authority of Singapore (MPA)

name: Pasir Panjang Terminal
geopoliticalNotes: Currently the most advanced and busiest container terminal complex in Singapore. It is capable of handling the world's largest ultra-large container vessels (ULCVs) and is the backbone of Singapore's transshipment economy.
notes: Highly automated terminal featuring remote-controlled quay cranes. Scheduled for consolidation into Tuas Port by 2040.

name: Jurong Port
geopoliticalNotes: Singapore's primary gateway for general and bulk cargo, handling 90% of the nation's cement and steel imports. It is critical for the country's construction industry and domestic infrastructure development.
notes: Operates the world's largest common-user cement terminal and is increasingly diversifying into offshore and marine services.

name: Jurong Island Terminals
geopoliticalNotes: One of the world's leading energy and chemical hubs. The port facilities on Jurong Island are vital for global energy trade and the supply of high-value chemical products. It integrates refining, petrochemicals, and logistics in a single location.
notes: Includes the Jurong Island Terminal (JIT) for barge operations and numerous private jetties for oil majors.

name: Pulau Bukom Oil Terminal
geopoliticalNotes: A dedicated island refinery and terminal for Shell. It is a key node in Asian energy markets and critical for the storage and distribution of refined petroleum products across the region.
notes: One of Shell's most important refining and chemicals sites globally.

name: Changi Naval Base
geopoliticalNotes: The premier strategic defense hub in Southeast Asia. It is the only regional port capable of docking US aircraft carriers, making it a critical facility for US naval logistics and power projection in the Indo-Pacific.
notes: Co-located with the Republic of Singapore Navy (RSN) headquarters.

name: Sembawang Wharves
geopoliticalNotes: Handles specialized bulk cargo and serves as a major logistical base for international naval forces. It hosts the US Navy's COMLOG WESTPAC, providing essential support for regional maritime security.
notes: Known for handling timber and heavy equipment in addition to its military logistical role.

name: Tuas Naval Base
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically monitors the western approaches to the Singapore Strait. It is essential for protecting Singapore's territorial waters and ensuring the security of the vital sea lines of communication (SLOCs).
notes: Home to the RSN's patrol vessels, frigates, and littoral mission vessels.

name: Laem Chabang Port
geopoliticalNotes: Thailand’s primary deep-sea gateway and heart of the Eastern Economic Corridor (EEC).
notes: Handles over 70% of national container trade; major transshipment hub for the Mekong region.

name: Bangkok Port (Klong Toey)
geopoliticalNotes: Historic river port serving the capital's urban economy; vital for SME trade and domestic consumption.
notes: Currently undergoing modernization into a 'Smart Port' to reduce city congestion.

name: Map Ta Phut Industrial Port
geopoliticalNotes: Thailand’s largest industrial and petrochemical hub; critical for national energy security.
notes: Handles crude oil, liquid chemicals, and natural gas for the Eastern Seaboard.

name: Sriracha Harbour
geopoliticalNotes: Thailand’s first private international deep-sea port; serves automotive, steel, and manufacturing sectors.
notes: Provides flexible alternatives to state-run facilities; high specialized volume.

name: Songkhla Port
geopoliticalNotes: Primary maritime hub for Southern Thailand; main outlet for rubber and frozen seafood exports.
notes: Key trade link with Malaysia and Singapore.

name: Ranong Port
geopoliticalNotes: Gateway to the Andaman Sea and BIMSTEC region; western terminus for the proposed Thai Land Bridge.
notes: Strategic node intended to bypass the Malacca Strait in the future.

name: Sattahip Commercial Port
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic dual-use facility; main base for the Royal Thai Navy and commercial Ro-Ro cargo for the EEC.
notes: Significant for regional security and heavy machinery logistics.

name: Phuket Deep Sea Port
geopoliticalNotes: Primary Andaman gateway for the tourism industry; strategically essential for Southern tourism supply chain.
notes: Focuses on international cruise connectivity and regional tourism logistics.

name: Ambarli Port
geopoliticalNotes: Turkey's largest container port. Serves the massive Istanbul industrial and consumer market; essential gateway for trade with Europe.
notes: Complex of several terminals including Kumport, Mardas, and Akçansa.

name: Aliaga Port
geopoliticalNotes: Major petrochemical and ship-breaking hub. Fastest-growing container base for Aegean manufacturers.
notes: Home to Petkim (petrochemicals) and several private container terminals like Nemport and TCE Ege.

name: Mersin International Port
geopoliticalNotes: Primary gateway for southern and central Turkey. Strategic transit point for trade with Iraq and the broader Middle East.
notes: Operated by Mersin International Port (MIP), a joint venture between PSA International and Akfen.

name: Kocaeli Port
geopoliticalNotes: Turkey's industrial heartland. Handles major automotive, chemical, and heavy machinery exports.
notes: Includes several terminals in Izmit Bay, such as DP World Yarimca and Evyap Port.

name: Iskenderun Port
geopoliticalNotes: Critical for industrial bulk (steel and coal) and energy. Major Red Sea-Mediterranean logistics bridge link.
notes: Operated by LimakPort Iskenderun.

name: Izmir Port
geopoliticalNotes: Historical export gateway for agricultural and industrial goods from western Anatolia.
notes: Also known as Alsancak Port; state-owned and handles significant cruise traffic.

name: Samsun Port
geopoliticalNotes: Largest Black Sea port of Turkey. Key link to Eastern Europe and Central Asia via rail and Ro-Ro lines.
notes: Operated by Samsunport (Ceynak Group).

name: Trabzon Port
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic transit point for the historical Silk Road. Vital for trade with the Caucasus and Iran.
notes: Operated by Trabzonport (Albayrak Group).

name: Port of Pivdennyi
geopoliticalNotes: Deepest port in the Black Sea and Ukraine's primary export hub for iron ore and grain. Central to the Ukrainian Sea Corridor's success.
notes: Capable of handling Capesize vessels. Regularly targeted by missile strikes but remains operational.

name: Port of Chornomorsk
geopoliticalNotes: Primary hub for the Ukrainian Sea Corridor and container logistics. Essential for grain exports and ferry connections to the Mediterranean.
notes: Undergoing major concession tenders in 2025 to modernize terminal infrastructure.

name: Port of Odesa
geopoliticalNotes: Historic and strategic multi-purpose hub. Vital for Ukraine's overall maritime identity and global supply chain integration.
notes: Handles significant container, oil, and grain traffic. High frequency of air raid alerts in the region.

name: Port of Izmail
geopoliticalNotes: Ukraine's largest river port on the Danube. Served as a critical alternative route during the Black Sea blockade.
notes: Key for iron ore exports to EU steel plants. Draft is sensitive to river levels.

name: Port of Reni
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic Danube port at the junction of Ukraine, Romania, and Moldova. Critical for liquid bulk and fertilizers.
notes: Plays a vital role in the EU 'Solidarity Lanes' initiative.

name: Port of Mykolaiv
geopoliticalNotes: Major shipbuilding and grain export hub. Currently blocked due to proximity to front lines and the Kinburn Spit.
notes: Strategic value remains extremely high for post-war reconstruction and maritime security.

name: Port of Mariupol
geopoliticalNotes: Major industrial port on the Sea of Azov. Currently under Russian occupation and heavily damaged.
notes: Excluded from official Ukrainian statistics due to occupation.

name: Port of Berdiansk
geopoliticalNotes: Export hub for agricultural products on the Sea of Azov. Currently under Russian occupation.
notes: Strategic for regional logistics and control over the Azov coastline.

name: Port of Los Angeles
geopoliticalNotes: Known as 'America's Port,' it is the busiest container hub in the Western Hemisphere and the primary gateway for trans-Pacific trade with Asia. It is a critical node in the U.S. national supply chain, handling 17% of all U.S. waterborne trade.
notes: A Designated Strategic Seaport by the DoD. It features 25 terminals and encompasses 43 miles of waterfront.

sources.name: Port of Los Angeles Official Website

name: Port of Long Beach
geopoliticalNotes: The second busiest U.S. container port and a major gateway for trans-Pacific trade. Its deep-water berths (the deepest in the U.S.) allow it to handle the world's largest container ships, making it essential for high-volume consumer goods imports.
notes: A Designated Strategic Seaport and a global leader in 'Green Port' environmental initiatives.

sources.name: Port of Long Beach Official Website

name: Port of New York and New Jersey
geopoliticalNotes: The largest port on the U.S. East Coast and the primary hub for transatlantic trade with Europe. It serves over 60 million regional consumers and is critical for the economic stability of the Northeast corridor.
notes: A Designated Strategic Seaport consisting of a complex of six container terminals and multiple specialized cargo facilities.

name: Port of Savannah
geopoliticalNotes: The fastest-growing container port in the U.S. and a major export gateway for the American Southeast. Its single-terminal design maximizes operational efficiency for large-scale logistics operations.
notes: A Designated Strategic Seaport. The Garden City Terminal is the largest single-terminal container facility in North America.

name: Port of Houston
geopoliticalNotes: The world's leading hub for petrochemicals and refined oil products. It is the largest U.S. port by waterborne tonnage and is fundamental to U.S. energy security and the global export of liquefied natural gas (LNG) and plastics.
notes: A 25-mile-long complex of over 200 public and private terminals along the Houston Ship Channel.

name: Port of Virginia
geopoliticalNotes: The world's premier coal export hub and home to the deepest natural harbor on the U.S. East Coast. It is strategically vital due to its proximity to Naval Station Norfolk, the world's largest naval base.
notes: A Designated Strategic Seaport. Currently dredging to 55 feet to accommodate the next generation of ultra-large ships.

name: Northwest Seaport Alliance
geopoliticalNotes: A critical gateway for trade with Alaska and a major trans-Pacific hub for Asian imports and U.S. agricultural exports. It provides essential logistical support for the Pacific Northwest economy.
notes: A Designated Strategic Seaport formed by a marine cargo operating partnership between the Port of Seattle and the Port of Tacoma.

name: Port of Charleston
geopoliticalNotes: One of the deepest harbors in the Southeastern U.S. and a major export hub for the American automotive industry (e.g., BMW, Volvo). It is also significant for handling specialized DOE nuclear material shipments.
notes: A Designated Strategic Seaport. Recently completed a major deepening project to 52 feet.

name: Ho Chi Minh City Port (Cat Lai)
geopoliticalNotes: Vietnam's busiest port; handles approximately 50% of the nation's container volume. Vital for the Southern Key Economic Region.
notes: Economic engine of Vietnam; primary gateway for consumer goods.

name: Hai Phong Port (Lach Huyen)
geopoliticalNotes: Northern gateway; primary hub for electronics and manufacturing exports to China, US, and EU.
notes: Only northern port capable of receiving large mother vessels.

sources.name: Hai Phong Port Joint Stock Company

name: Cai Mep - Thi Vai Port Complex
geopoliticalNotes: Deep-water hub capable of receiving ultra-large vessels (24,000 TEU). Direct transpacific routes to US/EU.
notes: Strategically located to bypass transshipment hubs like Singapore for long-haul routes.

name: Da Nang Port (Tien Sa)
geopoliticalNotes: Terminus of the East-West Economic Corridor (EWEC), connecting Vietnam to Laos, Thailand, and Myanmar.
notes: Central Vietnam's primary gateway; high regional connectivity.

name: Quang Ninh Port (Cai Lan)
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic for coal, minerals, and agricultural trade with China. Key logistics hub for the Northeast region.
notes: Handles high volume of dry bulk and raw materials.

name: Quy Nhon Port
geopoliticalNotes: Crucial for agricultural exports from the Central Highlands and Southern Laos/Northeast Cambodia.
notes: Gateway for the Indochina crossroads.

name: Cam Ranh International Port
geopoliticalNotes: One of the world's finest natural deep-water harbors; dual-use (military/commercial) with high naval significance.
notes: Strategic defense node in the South China Sea.

name: Chu Lai Port
geopoliticalNotes: Developed by THACO; serves the Chu Lai Open Economic Zone. Key for automotive and high-tech supply chains.
notes: Industrial hub focused on logistics and manufacturing integration.

name: Port of Durban
geopoliticalNotes: Busiest port in Sub-Saharan Africa and primary container hub for South Africa. Gateway to Gauteng industrial heartland and SADC neighbors.
notes: Handles over 60% of South Africa's container traffic.

name: Port of Richards Bay
geopoliticalNotes: South Africa's premier bulk cargo port. Houses the Richards Bay Coal Terminal (RBCT), the largest coal export facility in Africa.
notes: Deep-water port designed for large-scale mineral exports.

name: Port of Cape Town
geopoliticalNotes: 'Gateway to the West' and primary hub for agricultural exports (deciduous fruit, wine). Major transshipment point between continents.
notes: Handles significant volumes of perishables and high-value cargo.

name: Port of Saldanha Bay
geopoliticalNotes: Largest and deepest natural port in the Southern Hemisphere. Dedicated export hub for iron ore and critical crude oil storage.
notes: Linked via 861km heavy-haul rail line to Sishen mines.

name: Port of Ngqura
geopoliticalNotes: South Africa's newest and deepest container port. Designed as a transshipment hub to relieve Durban and Cape Town.
notes: Integrated into Coega Special Economic Zone (SEZ); future manganese hub.

name: Port of Port Elizabeth
geopoliticalNotes: Major hub for the automotive industry (exporting vehicles and components). Vital for Eastern Cape manufacturing.
notes: Multi-cargo port; some bulk operations shifting to Ngqura.

name: Port of East London
geopoliticalNotes: South Africa's only commercial river port. Strategically vital for the automotive sector (Mercedes-Benz).
notes: Features the largest grain elevator in the country.

name: Port of Mossel Bay
geopoliticalNotes: Smallest commercial port, primarily serving the oil and gas industry. Base for PetroSA's offshore operations.
notes: Handles petroleum products for the Garden Route region.
